title: practical english usage - Part 217
content:
She doesn't like olives, and neither do I.

’ I've forgotten the address.’ ’ So have I.' Ellipsis also happens after so, neither and nor (►  309). Note the word order.

grammar •  280  ellipsis: infinitives Stay as long as you like.

Come when you want (to). I’ll do what I like.

these are used after certain conjunctions - for instance when, if, what, as.

However, to is often dropped after want, and almost always after like, when not (     ... I didn’t want.) My parents encouraged me to study art, but I didn’ t want to.

’ Are you interested in working abroad? ’ I’d like to. ’ (     ... I'd like.) choose.

We   cannot usually leave out to after would like/love/hate/prefer, want and 3 (would)      like,    want,      etc ’ Can you start the car? ’ I’ll try (to).’ It also happens after verbs which  can stand alone without a following infinitive.

You can’ t force him  to leave home if he’s not ready (to).

He’ll never leave home; he hasn’t got the courage (to).

and adjectives.

In some cases the whole infinitive can be left out. This happens after nouns 2 ellipsis     of  whole     infinitive:      I'll try.

You’ve got more freckles than you used to have, (     ¥oube-got more freckles not She hasn’t been promoted yet, but she ought to be. (     .. .-but she eugkt-to?) not There are more flowers than there used to be. (     ... than thereusedto.) Be and have (used for possession) are not usually dropped.
